title: Iterative LLM-driven de novo drug design with multi-method consensus scoring: a case study on EGFR T790M/L858R
abstract: We present a closed-loop de novo drug-design pipeline in which a large language model (Claude Opus 4.7) acts as the reasoning generator inside an iterative loop with classical physics-based filters (RDKit drug-likeness, PAINS, SA), AutoDock Vina docking, MM-GBSA rescoring (amber14 + SMIRNOFF + OBC2), and short implicit-solvent molecular dynamics for pose-stability triage. Unlike generative neural networks, the LLM emits a structured rationale per proposal — making the chemistry behind every design auditable. Applied to EGFR T790M/L858R (PDB 4ZAU) over three feedback rounds with 60 proposals, the pipeline produced 27 unique candidates with docking scores ranging from −8.0 to −9.91 kcal/mol. A re-dock against wild-type EGFR (PDB 1M17) followed by single-trajectory MM-GBSA on the top five hit

title: A modular and affordable self-driving laboratory enables vision-guided optimization of metal electrodeposition
abstract: Metal electrodeposition is a widely used materials synthesis technique; however, industrial applications often require optimization of complex precursor formulations and electrodeposition parameters, which is typically performed in a slow, inefficient, and empirical manner. Self-driving labs (SDLs) that combine automated electrodeposition with machine-learning-guided decision-making could accelerate discovery in these complex, high-dimensional parameter spaces, but they face practical challenges. Notably, automated electrodeposition platforms need a continuous supply of pristine electrode surfaces, preservation and logging of electrodeposited samples, and the ability to easily incorporate non-electrochemical, downstream characterization. To overcome these challenges, we developed a modular

title: Human Body as a Model for Distributed Intelligence in Self-Driving Laboratories
abstract: Self-driving laboratories are emerging as a transformative paradigm for accelerating scientific discovery through the integration of artificial intelligence, automation, and robotics. However, the intrinsic complexity and unpredictability of experimental environments challenge the feasibility of fully centralized laboratory control architectures. In this perspective, we propose the human body as a conceptual model for the design of distributed-intelligence self-driving laboratories. Inspired by the organization of the nervous system, we distinguish two complementary levels of control: a high-level cognitive layer responsible for reasoning, planning, and scientific decision-making, and a distributed adaptive layer responsible for local operational optimization, sensing, and real-time reacti

title: Multi-task scheduling of self-driving laboratories under scientific constraints
abstract: Self-driving laboratories (SDLs) integrate automation, robotics, and artificial intelligence to autonomously execute scientific experiments. As SDLs evolve toward concurrent multi-task execution and support for heterogeneous experiments, experimental scheduling becomes a critical decision-making layer, determining the temporal order and execution timing of operations under strict scientific constraints, including operation precedence, station allocation, batch processing, experimental parameters, and critically, time synchronization between consecutive operations. For example, in inorganic synthesis, temporal deviations during nucleation can fundamentally alter material properties. While SDLs offer a promising route toward accelerated discovery, the absence of explicit scheduling for concu

title: Multi-task scheduling for chemical processes in self-driving laboratories under scientific constraints
abstract: Self-driving laboratories (SDLs) integrate process automation, robotics, and artificial intelligence (AI) to accelerate chemical discovery. As SDLs evolve toward concurrent multi-task execution, experimental scheduling becomes a critical decision-making layer, determining the ordering and timing of operations under strict scientific constraints, including operation precedence, station allocation, batch processing, experimental parameters, and critically, time synchronization between consecutive operations. For example, in inorganic synthesis, temporal deviations during nucleation can fundamentally alter material properties. The absence of scheduling for concurrent chemical processes leads to resource conflicts and uncontrolled interruptions, undermining the reproducibility and data quality

title: Bridging decision-making engines and workflow design in self-driving laboratories: A NIMO–IvoryOS integration study
abstract: Self-driving laboratories (SDLs) have the potential to accelerate scientific discovery, but their development often requires substantial programming effort. To lower this barrier, we present an integrated orchestration framework that combines NIMO and IvoryOS. In this framework, NIMO provides decision-making algorithms for autonomous experimentation, while IvoryOS offers an intuitive web interface automatically generated from Python code. We demonstrate the utility of this integration through an automated study of the coffee ring effect. The proposed framework offers a practical pathway toward more accessible SDLs.

title: Artificial Coater: An In Situ Self-Driving Lab Reveals Design Rules and Mechanisms for Ambient Perovskite Film Fabrication
abstract: Ambient solution-processing of metal halide perovskites remains highly sensitive to subtle variations in environment, formulation, and human technique, often leading to unpredictable outcomes and limited mechanistic understanding. Despite rapid advances in materials and device performance, researchers continue to rely heavily on manual and intuition-driven optimization, inconsistent fabrication protocols, and subjective film quality evaluation. These challenges persist across diverse coating methods, including but not limited to antisolvent dripping, which is especially sensitive to ambient manufacturing. In this work, we present the Artificial Coater, a self-driving laboratory platform that integrates closed-loop Bayesian optimization, in situ UV-Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy, an

title: ChemVLMate: a multimodal, dual-arm, seeing robotic chemist for adaptive synthesis
abstract: Chemical synthesis is a dynamic process. Chemists rely on a perception-reasoning-manipulation loop to interpret evolving experimental environments and states and adapt operations real-time. Yet most robotic chemistry platforms remain perception-blind, executing static scripts that assume nominal behaviours and fixed environments. Here we introduce ChemVLMate, a multimodal dualarm robotic chemist that closes the perception–reasoning–manipulation loop by grounding decision making in real-time vision. An open-vocabulary vision–language model converts an overhead image into a structured semantic observation of tools, reagents and instrument readouts; a language-model policy selects the next parameterized laboratory action from a constrained API; and coordinated bimanual manipulation executes i

title: A reaction-aware autonomous platform co-optimizing formulation and execution for the synthesis of zeolitic imidazolate framework
Molecular Pinball: A Deterministic Chemistry Environment for Benchmarking Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
abstract: The paradigm shift from empirical reward modeling to rule-based verification has redefined modern reinforcement learning research. Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a cornerstone methodology for developing sophisticated reasoning capabilities in autonomous agents, circumventing the limitations of human-annotated preference data. By leveraging deterministic reward signals—such as the execution correctness of code or the logical validity of a mathematical proof—RLVR enables agents to learn through unconstrained exploration, implicitly incentivizing coherent, step-by-step reasoning. However, the application of RLVR has historically been restricted to purely textual, mathematical, or programmatic domains. The physical sciences, where reasoning requires naviga

title: Q-planner: a harness system for quantum chemistry agents
abstract: LLM-based agents for computational chemistry commonly place the model inside an accumulating execution loop, causing context growth proportional to workflow length, unpredictable token costs, and degraded plan reliability at scale. We present a chemistry computation harness that confines LLM invocations to three semantically bounded stages—compound identification, planning, and reporting—while delegating all tool dispatch, parallel scheduling, arithmetic derivation, and artifact routing to a deterministic LangGraph executor that consumes zero LLM tokens during execution; a library of domain skill modules injects specialist quantum chemistry protocols into the planner context at planning time without enlarging the execution context. Across five representative quantum chemistry workflows, av

title: ChemAgents: A Hybrid Agentic Framework Coupling Large Language Models, Molecular Transformers, and Physics-Based Simulations for Accessible Computational Chemistry
abstract: The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence in chemistry has produced a powerful yet fragmented ecosystem of predictive models, creating a significant accessibility gap for domain experts who lack specialized machine learning expertise. To bridge this divide, we introduce ChemAgents, a practical, domain-specific agentic AI framework designed to make molecular property prediction and simulation more intuitive and reproducible. Expanding upon previous conversational agent architectures such as ChemCrow [1] and ChemToolAgent [2], ChemAgents employs a layered system of 13 specialized agents, a custom toolset integrating PubChem, LAMMPS molecular dynamics, and a novel Simulation-AI Coupler, alongside an aggressive duallayer caching architecture (in-memory LRU + persistent SQLite) that redu

title: Predictive Circular Chemistry: A Computational Framework for Engineering End-of-Life Fate into Chemical Formulations from First Principles
abstract: The chemical formulation industry generates enormous volumes of post-consumer waste whose environmental fate is largely determined at the design stage-yet end-of-life (EoL) fate prediction has historically been absent from formulation optimization workflows. This article introduces Predictive Circular Chemistry (PCC), a computational design paradigm that treats environmental EoL fate as a first-class optimization objective, coequal with performance, cost, and regulatory compliance. We describe the Circular Formulation Engine (CFE), a module within the IntelliForm AI platform that operationalizes PCC via a four-model EoL fate stack (BIOWIN3/6, ECOSAR v2.2, AopWin v1.92, and Koc-GUS leachate mobility), a composite Waste Score metric, and tight integration with the NSGA-III multi-objective ev

title: Mono-material polydisulfides for pressure sensitive adhesive tapes with closed-loop recyclability
abstract: Pressure-sensitive adhesives (PSAs) are indispensable components across all sectors of modern society, ranging from packaging to aerospace or construction. However, conventional PSAs are non-recyclable multi-layer materials that contain numerous additives, and they often hamper the recycling of other materials such as plastics or metals when they are applied. Furthermore, most research on sustainable PSA development has focused on the adhesive layer with less attention on the non-adhesive plastic backing layer which typically uses petroleum-derived plastics. Herein, we report a strategy to create ‘mono-material’ PSA tapes from biogenic polydisulfide copolymers where both the plastic backing and adhesive layer are chemically recyclable. title: True Closed-Loop Recyclable Hydrogels enabled by Imine Boronic Ester Crosslinking
abstract: The relationship between molecular structure and macroscopic function is a foundational principle in materials science, in which subtle molecular variations produce pronounced differences in strength, stiffness, and elasticity. In hydrogels, replacing static covalent bonds with dynamic covalent bonds (DCBs) creates newfound capabilities, including selfhealing and recyclability. Herein, how substitutional differences in dual DCB imine boronic ester crosslinkers, together with matrix pH, influence hydrogel properties was investigated. A comparison of orthoand para-imine boronic esters showed the formation of 3-amino-benzoxaborole heterocycles in hydrogels derived from 2-formylphenylboronic acid. title: Closed-loop, Human-AI–driven optimization of geological hydrogen production
abstract: Geological hydrogen produced from iron-rich rocks via serpentinization is an emerging low-carbon energy pathway. However, reaction rates remain slow and difficult to control due to the coupling of multiple parameters and the complexity of rock-fluid interactions. Here we report a closed-loop, high-throughput experimental platform that integrates domain knowledge, Bayesian optimization and human-in-the-loop decision making to navigate and control the high-dimensional reaction landscape governing geological hydrogen production. By systematically exploring a nine-dimensional parameter space, the platform converges to high-production conditions in fewer than 90 experiments, achieving hydrogen concentrations up to two orders of magnitude higher than baseline rock–water reactions. Agentic Retrieval for Bio-Based Recovery of Critical and Near-Critical Minerals
abstract: Biological extraction routes are promising for critical-minerals recovery. We present a Large Language Model (LLM)-assisted autonomous literature mining agent that converts a large local PDF corpus into a schema-validated evidence dataset. The agent maintains persistent state in SQLite, applies staged gating, and invokes Claude LLM only on a small shortlist for figure/table-aware extraction. In one run, the pipeline produced 56 validated records. The dataset is dominated by bioleaching and biosorption, frequently targeting Cu, Ni, Zn, Co, and Fe. In the bioleaching of Cu subset (N=16), an analysis shows a dominant ore/tailing-bacterial Fe/S oxidizers-inorganic acid/ferric pathway plus smaller waste-context pathways with organic-acid signatures. This work demonstrates a cost-efficient pathw

title: «Just ask»: Agentic Pilot to empower chemists
abstract: Large Language Model agents capable of using software tools have recently surged in capability and popularity in the data science domain, but remain under-used in the physical sciences. We develop Agentic Pilot, a visual user interface built on LangGraph, to help fill this gap. Agentic Pilot enables the intuitive development of complex agentic workflows, allowing even small LLMs to perform industrially relevant tasks such as executing computational screening calculations or querying electronic lab notebook databases via natural language. In this work, we present Agentic Pilot and provide an industrial perspective on the future of LLM-based agentic workflows in chemistry.

title: Lessons Learned from the 2025 Agentic AI for Science Hackathon
abstract: The rapid emergence of agentic AI presents new opportunities and challenges for accelerating scientific discovery through tool-augmented reasoning, autonomous workflows, and reproducible results. To explore these capabilities in a hands-on, community-driven setting, we hosted the Agentic AI for Science Hackathon 2025, which attracted 352 registered participants. The event was designed to benchmark, test, and extend agentic AI workflows across scientific tasks while remaining modelagnostic and lowering the barrier to entry for participants from diverse backgrounds. Participants engaged with a unified API ecosystem centered on the fully open-access AtomGPT.org API (AGAPI), implementing tool calling, asynchronous agents, and multi-model reasoning to solve problems spanning materials database

title: Transparent Reporting for Agentic Catalysis Enabled by Artificial Intelligence (TRACE-AI): Community Guidelines and A Publication Checklist
abstract:

While these closed-loop capabilities hold promise to accelerate knowledge generation and technological innovation, they inevitably introduce new sources of variability in data lineage, model specification, and agent policies that can undermine FAIRness, rigor, and reproducibility. title: From Tools to Autonomous Agents: Rethinking AI-Driven Business Transformation
abstract: Organizations are accelerating investments in artificial intelligence (AI) to improve efficiency, enhance decision-making, and strengthen customer engagement. While generative AI and large language models (LLMs) have delivered productivity gains by augmenting knowledge work and automating discrete tasks, enterprise transformation remains uneven. Many firms report incremental improvements without sustained strategic advantage. This gap suggests that AI-driven transformation depends less on model sophistication than on how AI capabilities are embedded, coordinated, and governed within organizational systems.
